title: Diagnosing NLP Failures
concept: NLP debugging requires separating code bugs, data preprocessing bugs, tokenizer corruption, and model capacity limits.

exercises.prompt: Why is it important to separate code bugs from data and tokenizer bugs in NLP?

options.text: Yes, it helps pinpoint the exact failure point.
No, you should always just buy a bigger model.
No, all NLP bugs are solved by cleaning data.

exercises.explain: Isolating issues step-by-step prevents you from wasting time tuning model sizes when the real problem is just a broken text importer.

cards.title: Why is NLP Debugging Hard?
cards.body: When a computer vision model fails, it might mistake a dog for a cat. But when an NLP model fails, it could output total nonsense, ignore negative words, or crash completely. How do you find the needle in the haystack when text processing goes wrong? Let's learn how to diagnose these tricky language bugs!

cards.title: The Anatomy of an NLP Failure
cards.body: When an AI NLP system fails, it is rarely just one big mystery. Debugging requires isolating the failure into four distinct buckets: code bugs (errors in your loops or layers), data preprocessing bugs (mismangled text or bad cleaning), tokenizer corruption (words being sliced up incorrectly), and model capacity limits (the model is simply too small to learn the task).

cards.title: The Bookshelf Blueprint
cards.body: Imagine building a custom bookshelf. If the shelves collapse, you must figure out the root cause. Did you misread the instructions (code bug)? Did you use rotten wood (bad data)? Did you cut the pieces to the wrong scale (tokenizer error)? Or is the wood simply too weak to hold heavy encyclopedias (model capacity limit)? Debugging NLP is exactly like this troubleshooting process.

cards.title: Real-World Failures
cards.body:
Let's say your chatbot suddenly starts outputting gibberish. 

• Code bug: You accidentally shuffled the target words during training.
• Data bug: Your text importer replaced all apostrophes with weird symbols like 'Ã©'.
• Tokenizer bug: Your tokenizer was trained on English but you are feeding it French text.
• Capacity limit: Your tiny 1-million parameter model cannot remember long paragraphs.

cards.title: Is it Always the Model's Fault?
cards.body: It is easy to assume that if a model gets 0% accuracy, the neural network structure is broken. In reality, most NLP failures are caused by messy data preprocessing (like accidentally erasing all punctuation) or tokenizer mismatches, not the model's actual brain power!

cards.title: Parser vs. Processor
cards.body: Comparing two distinct types of NLP failures to understand where to look for the fix.

left.text: The word 'don't' is split into 'do' and 'n't', but your vocabulary only knows 'do'. Meaning is lost!

right.text: The text is processed perfectly, but the model is too tiny to remember context from 10 pages ago.

exercises.prompt: Your code runs perfectly, the data is clean, and the tokens look correct. However, your model is too small to remember long paragraphs of text. What limit have you hit?
exercises.explain: If the code runs without errors but the model simply lacks the memory or power to learn complex patterns, you have hit a model capacity limit. You need a larger model.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Data preprocessing bugs and tokenizer corruption are the exact same issue and should be debugged together.
exercises.explain: Data cleaning bugs (like stripping out question marks) happen before tokenization, while tokenizer bugs happen when splitting the remaining text into pieces. They are distinct debugging steps.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the troubleshooting concept.
exercises.sentence: If your model sees words turned into random fragments, you have a mismatch in your tokenizer.
exercises.explain: The tokenizer is responsible for slicing raw text strings into numerical IDs. If it fails, the model receives garbage inputs.

exercises.prompt: Which statement about NLP debugging is FALSE?

options.text: A code bug will often cause a crash, whereas a data preprocessing bug might run silently but output poor results.
If your raw text data is perfectly clean, it is impossible to have tokenizer corruption issues.
A model capacity limit means the AI is simply not complex enough to learn the nuances of your text task.

exercises.explain: The second statement is incorrect because clean text is useless if the tokenizer cuts it up into unrecognizable fragments (like breaking 'unbelievable' into 'un-be-lie-vable' instead of keeping its semantic root).

exercises.prompt: Your smart oven assistant fails to understand the recipe step 'preheat the oven'. You discover the word 'preheat' is split into '[UNK]' (unknown text symbols) before entering the model. What type of NLP failure is this?

options.text: A code bug in the neural network layer
A tokenizer mismatch causing out-of-vocabulary loss
An overfitting error on the training dataset

exercises.explain: A tokenizer converting 'preheat' to 'unknown' destroys the word's meaning before it ever reaches the neural network. This is a classic preprocessing/tokenizer failure rather than a bug in the model's layers or code logic.
